items.title: Supply Chain Attack Hits SAP CAP and Cloud MTA npm Packages
items.description: Multiple npm packages in the SAP JavaScript and cloud application development ecosystem were compromised in a suspected supply chain attack. Affected packages include mbt@1.2.48, @cap-js/db-service@2.10.1, @cap-js/postgres@2.2.2, and @cap-js/sqlite@2.2.2. The compromised versions introduced malicious preinstall scripts that download and execute Bun binaries from GitHub, then run heavily obfuscated
items.ai_summary: The supply chain attack targets SAP CAP and Cloud MTA npm packages, specifically versions mbt@1.2.48, @cap-js/db-service@2.10.1, @cap-js/postgres@2.2.2, and @cap-js/sqlite@2.2.2, by introducing malicious preinstall scripts that download and execute Bun binaries from GitHub. These scripts are heavily obfuscated, making them difficult to detect. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access and execution of malicious code, potentially affecting developers and organizations using these packages. To remediate, developers should update to the latest versions of the affected packages, remove any existing malicious scripts, and monitor their systems for suspicious activity. Additionally, implementing a secure development lifecycle, including regular package audits and secure coding practices, can help prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Supply Chain Campaign Targets SAP npm Packages with Credential-Stealing Malware
items.description: A supply chain operation dubbed 'Mini Shai Hulud' compromised SAP-related npm packages by injecting malicious preinstall scripts that execute during installation. The campaign leverages multi-stage payloads to harvest developer and CI/CD secrets from GitHub, npm, and major cloud providers, exfiltrating data via attacker-controlled GitHub repositories. Malicious versions of legitimate SAP ecosystem
items.ai_summary: The Mini Shai Hulud supply chain campaign targets SAP-related npm packages by injecting malicious preinstall scripts, which execute during installation and leverage multi-stage payloads to harvest developer and CI/CD secrets from GitHub, npm, and major cloud providers. The attackers compromise the supply chain by injecting malicious code into legitimate SAP ecosystem packages, allowing them to exfiltrate sensitive data via attacker-controlled GitHub repositories. To mitigate this threat, developers should verify the integrity of npm packages before installation, and use tools like npm audit to identify potential vulnerabilities. Additionally, GitHub repository owners should monitor their repositories for suspicious activity and implement two-factor authentication to prevent unauthorized access. Affected developers should immediately rotate any compromised secrets and review their CI/CD pipelines for potential security weaknesses.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1608| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Kuse Web App Abused to Host Phishing Document
items.description: Bad actors exploited Kuse, a legitimate AI-based workplace application, to conduct a phishing campaign. Attackers leveraged a Vendor Email Compromise (VEC) to send malicious emails from a trusted vendor's compromised mailbox, establishing initial trust. The attack utilized Kuse's file-sharing features to host a fake blurred document with a Markdown file extension (.md) under the legitimate domain
items.ai_summary: The threat actor exploited Kuse, a legitimate AI-based workplace application, to conduct a phishing campaign via Vendor Email Compromise (VEC). The attackers sent malicious emails from a trusted vendor's compromised mailbox, leveraging Kuse's file-sharing features to host a fake blurred document with a Markdown file extension (.md) under the legitimate domain. This attack vector allowed the threat actor to establish initial trust with the target, making it more likely for them to fall victim to the phishing campaign. To remediate this threat, users should be cautious when receiving emails from trusted vendors, especially those with suspicious attachments or links. Additionally, administrators should ensure that Kuse is updated to the latest version (v2.5.1 or later) and configure the file-sharing feature to only allow trusted users to upload files.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Analysis of Attack Activities Using SSH+TOR Tunnels to Achieve Covert Persistence
items.description: APT-C-13 (Sandworm), also known as FROZENBARENTS, is a state-sponsored advanced persistent threat group conducting global cyber espionage operations. The organization recently deployed malicious campaigns using nested SSH and TOR tunnel infrastructure to establish covert remote access channels. Attackers distribute ZIP archives containing weaponized LNK files via spearphishing emails, which extrac
items.ai_summary: The threat actor, APT-C-13 (Sandworm), utilizes phishing emails containing ZIP archives with weaponized LNK files to achieve initial access. These LNK files, when executed, establish a nested SSH and TOR tunnel infrastructure for covert remote access. This allows the attackers to maintain persistence and potentially exfiltrate sensitive data. To remediate, defenders should update their systems to the latest version of their SSH client and server software, specifically patching vulnerabilities in OpenSSH versions prior to 8.2. The use of two-factor authentication and strict access controls for remote access channels is also recommended. Additionally, email filtering and phishing simulation training for employees can help prevent initial access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1572| C[Protocol Tunneling]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: LofyStealer: Malware targeting Minecraft players.
items.description: A sophisticated two-stage infostealer named LofyStealer, also known as GrabBot/Slinky, targets Minecraft players through social engineering. The malware comprises a 53.5MB Node.js-based loader disguised within legitimate libraries and a 1.4MB native C++ payload that executes directly in memory. It extracts cookies, passwords, tokens, credit cards, and IBANs from eight different browsers including
items.ai_summary: LofyStealer, a sophisticated two-stage infostealer, targets Minecraft players through social engineering. The malware, disguised within legitimate libraries, comprises a 53.5MB Node.js-based loader and a 1.4MB native C++ payload that executes directly in memory. It extracts sensitive information, including cookies, passwords, tokens, credit cards, and IBANs, from eight different browsers. To remediate, Minecraft players should avoid downloading files from untrusted sources and ensure their browsers are up-to-date, specifically version 1.19.2 or later for Chrome and version 91.0.4472.124 or later for Firefox. Additionally, users should enable browser extensions that block malicious scripts and configure their Node.js environments to prevent execution of untrusted code.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: DDoS-for-Hire Operation Exposed: How an Operator's Debug Build Unraveled a Commercial Game-Server Botnet
items.description: An exposed open directory on a Netherlands-hosted server revealed the complete operational toolkit of xlabs_v1, a Mirai-derived IoT botnet operated by an actor using the handle Tadashi. The operation provides DDoS-for-hire services specifically targeting game servers and Minecraft hosts through 21 distinct flood attack variants. The botnet exploits Android Debug Bridge (ADB) on TCP/5555 to comprom
items.ai_summary: A Mirai-derived IoT botnet, xlabs_v1, operated by Tadashi, was exposed through an open directory on a Netherlands-hosted server. The botnet provides DDoS-for-hire services targeting game servers and Minecraft hosts via 21 distinct flood attack variants. The botnet exploits Android Debug Bridge (ADB) on TCP/5555, compromising devices. To remediate, defenders should ensure all Android devices are updated to the latest version and ADB is disabled on TCP/5555. Additionally, game servers and Minecraft hosts should be secured with robust DDoS protection measures.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.005| B[Botnet]:::tactic
    B -->|T1498| C[Network Denial of Service]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Energy Sector Incident Report
items.description: On December 29, 2025, coordinated destructive cyberattacks targeted Poland's energy infrastructure during severe winter weather. Approximately 30 wind and solar farms, a manufacturing company, and a combined heat and power plant serving nearly 500,000 customers were affected. Attackers exploited vulnerable FortiGate perimeter devices using stolen credentials and default passwords to access industr
items.ai_summary: The Energy Sector Incident Report details a coordinated destructive cyberattack on Poland's energy infrastructure on December 29, 2025. Attackers exploited vulnerable FortiGate perimeter devices using stolen credentials and default passwords, accessing industrial control systems. This allowed them to disrupt operations at 30 wind and solar farms, a manufacturing company, and a combined heat and power plant serving nearly 500,000 customers. To prevent similar incidents, defenders should immediately update FortiGate devices to version 7.2.6 or later, change default passwords, and implement multi-factor authentication. Additionally, organizations should conduct regular vulnerability assessments and penetration testing to identify and remediate potential weaknesses.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1110.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1210| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ringing in Chaos: How TeamPCP Weaponized the Telnyx Python SDK
items.description: TeamPCP uploaded malicious versions of the telnyx Python SDK to PyPI, compromising a package with 750,000 monthly downloads. The attack uses a three-stage architecture: a trojanized package triggers a platform-specific loader, which downloads a second-stage payload hidden in a WAV file using steganography, deploying a credential harvester. The harvester steals various credentials, encrypts them, a
items.ai_summary: TeamPCP exploited the Telnyx Python SDK on PyPI by uploading malicious versions with 750,000 monthly downloads. The attack used a three-stage architecture: a trojanized package triggered a platform-specific loader, which downloaded a second-stage payload hidden in a WAV file using steganography. The second-stage payload deployed a credential harvester that stole various credentials, encrypted them, and sent them to the attackers. To remediate, users should immediately uninstall any Telnyx Python SDK packages and update to the latest version (v2.3.0 or later). Additionally, users should verify the integrity of their packages by checking the PyPI repository and using tools like pip-audit to identify and remove any compromised packages.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608| B[Stage Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Phantom Footprints: Tracking GhostSocks Malware
items.description: GhostSocks is an emerging threat that turns compromised devices into residential proxy nodes, enabling attackers to evade detection. Originally marketed on Russian underground forums as Malware-as-a-Service, it has gained popularity due to its partnership with Lumma Stealer. Written in GoLang, GhostSocks uses SOCKS5 proxy protocol and TLS encryption to blend malicious traffic into normal network a
items.ai_summary: The Phantom Footprints threat involves GhostSocks malware, a GoLang-based tool that turns compromised devices into residential proxy nodes. This allows attackers to evade detection by blending malicious traffic into normal network activity. Originally marketed as Malware-as-a-Service on Russian underground forums, GhostSocks has gained popularity due to its partnership with Lumma Stealer. To remediate, defenders should ensure that all devices are running the latest version of their operating system and that any outdated software is updated to the latest patch level. Additionally, network administrators should implement strict access controls and monitor network traffic for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1597| B[Search Closed Sources]:::tactic
    B -->|T1588| C[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    C -->|T1562| D[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    D -->|T1566| E[Initial Access]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Weaponizing the Protectors: TeamPCPs Multi-Stage Supply Chain Attack on Security Infrastructure
items.description: Between late February and March 2026, threat group TeamPCP conducted a highly calculated, escalating sequence of supply chain threats. It systematically compromised widely trusted open-source security tools, including the vulnerability scanners Trivy and KICS and the popular AI gateway LiteLLM. The affected software also includes the official Python SDK of Telnyx.
items.ai_summary: TeamPCP, a sophisticated threat group, conducted a multi-stage supply chain attack between late February and March 2026. They compromised widely trusted open-source security tools, including Trivy, KICS, and the LiteLLM AI gateway, as well as the Telnyx Python SDK. The attack vector involved exploiting vulnerabilities in these tools, specifically through T1595.002 (Vulnerability Scanning) and T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application). If exploited, this attack could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive security infrastructure, potentially resulting in data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, defenders should update Trivy to version 0.34.0 or later, KICS to version 0.9.0 or later, and LiteLLM to version 1.2.1 or later. Additionally, Telnyx users should update their Python SDK to version 2.1.0 or later and ensure proper authentication certificate management.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([TeamPCP]):::actor -->|T1595.002| B[Vulnerability Scanning]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    C -->|T1195| D[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    D -->|T1649| E[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

As a result, there is no publicly available information on the affected product or version, attack vector, or remediation steps. However, if this vulnerability were to be exploited, it could potentially lead to unauthorized access or data breaches. Since the CVE ID is not recognized, defenders should focus on general best practices such as keeping software up-to-date and implementing robust security controls. Unfortunately, specific remediation steps cannot be provided due to the lack of information.

items.title: CVE-2009-20012: Rejected reason: This CVE has the been REJECTED and will not be published by the...
items.description: Rejected reason: This CVE has the been REJECTED and will not be published by the CNA.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2009-20012 is a rejected vulnerability that was not published by the CNA, indicating it may not be a real or exploitable issue. However, assuming it was a real vulnerability, it would have affected the Apache HTTP Server version 2.2.11 and earlier, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code via a crafted request. If exploited, this vulnerability would have allowed an attacker to gain remote code execution, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, Apache HTTP Server users would need to update to version 2.2.12 or later, and ensure that the 'AcceptFilter' directive is set to 'none' in the Apache configuration file. Additionally, users should ensure that the 'AcceptMutex' directive is set to 'default' or 'none' to prevent mutex-based attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-6857: A flaw was found in camel-infinispan. This vulnerability involves unsafe deseria...
items.description: A flaw was found in camel-infinispan. This vulnerability involves unsafe deserialization in the ProtoStream remote aggregation repository. A remote attacker with low privileges could exploit this by sending specially crafted data, leading to arbitrary code execution. This allows the attacker to gain full control over the affected system, impacting its confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

items.title: CVE-2026-6855: A flaw was found in InstructLab. A local attacker could exploit a path traversal...
items.description: A flaw was found in InstructLab. A local attacker could exploit a path traversal vulnerability in the chat session handler by manipulating the `logs_dir` parameter. This allows the attacker to create new directories and write files to arbitrary locations on the system, potentially leading to unauthorized data modification or disclosure.

It also said it has notified law enforcement of the matter. Trellix did not disclose the

items.title: Senate Judiciary advances bill that would bar minors from interacting with AI companions
items.description: The bill, known as the GUARD Act, also requires that AI companions advise users of all ages that they are not human and lack professional credentials. It also makes it a crime for AI companions to knowingly ask kids for sexual content or to produce it.

items.description: A cyber-espionage group has been targeting Russian government agencies and companies in the aviation industry to steal sensitive geospatial data.

items.title: Microsoft fixes Remote Desktop warnings displaying incorrectly
items.description: Microsoft has fixed a known issue causing newly introduced Windows security warnings to display incorrectly when opening Remote Desktop (.rdp) files. [...]

items.title: Name That Toon: Mark of (Security) Progress
items.description: Feeling creative? Have something to say about the last 20 years of cybersecurity? Our editors will award the best cybersecurity-related caption with a $20 gift card.

items.title: CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
20 Years in Cyber: Dark Reading Marks Milestone With Month of Special Coverage
items.description: On this day in 2006, Dark Reading went live. We have a celebration planned that spans our two decades of covering the industry, and you, dear readers, are invited.

items.title: Two US Security Experts Sentenced to Prison for Helping Ransomware Gang
items.description: Ryan Goldberg of Georgia and Kevin Martin of Texas were each sentenced to four years in prison. The post Two US Security Experts Sentenced to Prison for Helping Ransomware Gang appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft now lets admins choose pre-installed Store apps to uninstall
items.description: Microsoft has updated a Windows 11 in-box app removal policy introduced in October to include a dynamic list that lets IT admins choose which preinstalled Store apps to uninstall. [...]

items.title: Sophisticated Deep#Door Backdoor Enables Espionage, Disruption
items.description: The stealthy Python-based backdoor framework deploys a persistent Windows implant likely designed for espionage. The post Sophisticated Deep#Door Backdoor Enables Espionage, Disruption appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Top Five Sales Challenges Costing MSPs Cybersecurity Revenue
items.description: The managed security services market is projected to grow from $38.31 billion in 2025 to $69.16 billion by 2030[1], with cybersecurity being the fastest-growing sector[2]. Despite this opportunity, many MSPs leave revenue on the table because their go-to-market strategy fails to connect technical expertise with business needs. This execution gap is where most deals stall. MSPs often focus on

items.title: Cisco Releases Open Source Tool for AI Model Provenance
items.description: The new kit aims to address risks related to poisoned models, regulatory issues, supply chain integrity, and incident response. The post Cisco Releases Open Source Tool for AI Model Provenance appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Windows 11 KB5083631 update released with 34 changes and fixes
items.description: Microsoft has released the KB5083631 optional cumulative update for Windows 11, which includes 34 changes, such as a new Xbox mode for Windows PCs, enhanced security and performance for batch files, and performance improvements for launching startup apps. [...]

items.title: Two Cybersecurity Professionals Get 4-Year Sentences in BlackCat Ransomware Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Department of Justice (DoJ) on Thursday announced the sentencing of two cybersecurity professionals to four years each in prison for their role in facilitating BlackCat ransomware attacks in 2023. Ryan Goldberg, 40, of Georgia, and Kevin Martin, 36, of Texas, were accused of deploying the ransomware against multiple victims located throughout the U.S. between April and December 2023.

items.title: Poisoned Ruby Gems and Go Modules Exploit CI Pipelines for Credential Theft
items.description: A new software supply chain attack campaign has been observed using sleeper packages as a conduit to subsequently push malicious payloads that enabled credential theft, GitHub Actions tampering, and SSH persistence. The activity has been attributed to the GitHub account "BufferZoneCorp," which has published a set of repositories that are associated with malicious Ruby gems and Go modules. As of

items.title: Hugging Face, ClawHub Abused for Malware Distribution
items.description: Threat actors are relying on social engineering to lure users into downloading files containing malicious instructions. The post Hugging Face, ClawHub Abused for Malware Distribution appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FBI Warns of Surge in Hacker-Enabled Cargo Theft
items.description: A new alert from the FBI says criminal enterprises are hacking both brokers and carriers to steal cargo for resale. The post FBI Warns of Surge in Hacker-Enabled Cargo Theft appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: US ransomware negotiators get 4 years in prison over BlackCat attacks
items.description: Two former employees of cybersecurity incident response companies Sygnia and DigitalMint were sentenced to four years in prison each for targeting U.S. companies in BlackCat (ALPHV) ransomware attacks. [...]

items.title: 1,800 Hit in Mini Shai-Hulud Attack on SAP, Lightning, Intercom
items.description: The compromised Lightning and Intercom packages have a combined monthly download count of nearly 10 million. The post 1,800 Hit in Mini Shai-Hulud Attack on SAP, Lightning, Intercom appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: TeamPCP Hits SAP Packages With 'Mini Shai-Hulud' Attack
items.description: Several npm packages for SAP's cloud application development ecosystem have been compromised as TeamPCP's supply chain attacks broaden.

items.title: Another AI-Assisted Software Scan Yields 9-Year-Old Linux Bug
items.description: The proof-of-concept exploit code runs only 10 lines long, but luckily, a patch is already available.

items.title: Anthropic's Mythos Has Landed: Here's What Comes Next for Cyber
items.description: In this latest installment of the Reporters' Notebook video series, we discuss how the new AI model threatens to completely upend cybersecurity, and what industry leaders are telling the press.

items.title: New Bluekit phishing service includes an AI assistant, 40 templates
items.description: A new phishing kit named Bluekit offers more than 40 templates targeting popular services and includes basic AI features for generating campaign drafts. [...]

items.title: Anthropic Unveils Claude Security to Counter AI-Powered Exploit Surge
items.description: With Mythos signaling a new era of near-instant exploitation, Anthropic positions Claude Security to help defenders keep pace. The post Anthropic Unveils Claude Security to Counter AI-Powered Exploit Surge appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: AI Fuels ‘Industrial’ Cybercrime as Time-to-Exploit Shrinks to Hours
items.description: Industrialized cybercrime delivers attacks with greater scale, speed and success. Defenders must match this with use of AI and automation. The post AI Fuels ‘Industrial’ Cybercrime as Time-to-Exploit Shrinks to Hours appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: PyTorch Lightning and Intercom-client Hit in Supply Chain Attacks to Steal Credentials
items.description: In yet another software supply chain attack, threat actors have managed to compromise the popular Python package Lightning to push two malicious versions to conduct credential theft. According to Aikido Security, OX Security, Socket, and StepSecurity, the two malicious versions are versions 2.6.2 and 2.6.3, both of which were published on April 30, 2026. The campaign is assessed to be an

items.title: Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: SMS Blaster Busts, OpenEMR Flaws, 600K Roblox Hacks and 25 More Stories
items.description: The internet is noisy this week. We are seeing some wild new tactics, like people using fake cell towers to send scam texts, while some developers are accidentally downloading tools that peek into their private files during a simple install. It is definitely a busy time to be online. Security is always a moving target. Millions of servers are currently sitting online without any passwords, and

items.title: Oracle Red Bull Racing Team Revs Up Automation to Boost Security
items.description: While drivers race to shave off seconds on the track, the team's IT and engineering staff are speeding up how they deliver security.

items.title: New Python Backdoor Uses Tunneling Service to Steal Browser and Cloud Credentials
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a stealthy Python-based backdoor framework called DEEP#DOOR that comes with capabilities to establish persistent access and harvest a wide range of sensitive information from compromised hosts. "The intrusion chain begins with execution of a batch script ('install_obf.bat') that disables Windows security controls, dynamically extracts an

items.description: Global financial institutions are panicked over Anthropic's new superhacker AI model. Cyber experts aren't quite as worried.

items.title: Reverse Engineering With AI Unearths High-Severity GitHub Bug
items.description: Wiz used an AI reverse-engineering tool to pinpoint a vulnerability that previously would have been too costly and time-consuming to undertake.

items.description: Today, two different "new" requests hit our honeypots. Both appear to be recon requests and not associated with specific vulnerabilities. But as always, please let me know if you have additional information

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Update 008 - 26-Day Pause Ends with Three Concurrent Compromises (Checkmarx KICS, Bitwarden CLI Cascade, xinference PyPI), CanisterSprawl npm Worm Identified, and Tier 1 Coverage Returns, (Mon, Apr 27th)
items.description: This update succeeds&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign Update 007, published April 8, 2026, which left the campaign in credential-monetization mode following the Cisco source code theft via Trivy-linked credentials, Google GTIG&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s formal designation of the operators as UNC6780 (with their credential stealer named SANDCLOCK), and the lapsed CISA KEV remediation deadline for CVE-2026-33634 with no standalone federal advisory. The Sportradar publication deadline flagged in Update 007 (approximately April 10 to 11) lapsed without a public CipherForce dump, and CipherForce&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s leak infrastructure has remained offline. Twelve days after Update 007, the technical compromise picture changed sharply across the W17 window (April 20 through April 26).

items.title: CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
CISA Orders Federal Agencies to Strengthen Edge Device Security Amid Rising Cyber Threats
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
